title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1 Welcome
This Quarto book is a review workspace for the USACE Recreation Ontology and its related artifacts. It is designed to help subject matter experts, GIS staff, and ontology reviewers inspect the model structure, visualize relationships, and compare the ontology against supporting spatial and crosswalk materials.

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.1 Purpose
This site supports team review of the recreation ontology by organizing the core materials into a single navigable website.
The review package includes:

an overview of the ontology scope and modeling intent
an interactive visualization of ontology classes and properties
supporting spatial diagrams
the SDSFIE crosswalk material used for comparison and traceability

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.2 How to use this site
Recommended review flow:

Start with the Introduction for context and review goals.
Read the Ontology Overview to understand the model structure and intended interpretation.
Open Ontology Visualization to inspect class hierarchies and property relationships interactively.
Review the Spatial Diagrams for GIS-oriented context.
Review the SDSFIE Crosswalk to compare ontology terms with external structures.

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.3 Included source artifacts
This site is built from the following repository assets:

data/01-recreation-vocabulary.ttl
data/02-recreation-ontology.ttl
data/06-recreation-spatial.ttl
data/12-recreation-sdsfie-crosswalk.ttl

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.4 Reviewer guidance
When reviewing the ontology, focus on questions such as:

Are the class hierarchies intuitive and complete?
Do object properties connect the right kinds of entities?
Do datatype properties capture the operational attributes needed by users?
Are any labels, definitions, or modeling boundaries unclear?
Do the ontology and supporting diagrams align with expected recreation business concepts?

title: USACE Recreation Ontology Review
text:
1.5 Notes
This website is intended for collaborative review and discussion. It emphasizes readability and navigation over exhaustive RDF inspection.
Continue to the next chapter to begin the review.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.1 Review objective
This book presents the current draft of the USACE Recreation Ontology in a format that is easier to review than raw Turtle files alone. The goal is to support discussion of structure, naming, hierarchy, and relationship design before the ontology is finalized or expanded.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.2 What is being reviewed
The review focuses primarily on the ontology content in:

data/02-recreation-ontology.ttl

This ontology defines:

core recreation domain classes
class hierarchies
object properties connecting domain entities
datatype properties describing operational attributes

Additional files in this repository provide vocabulary, spatial context, and crosswalk material that support interpretation of the ontology.

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.3 Review perspective
This site is intended for a mixed audience, including:

ontology reviewers
GIS practitioners
program/domain subject matter experts
stakeholders validating terminology and structure

Because of that, the materials are organized to support both:

conceptual review, such as whether a class belongs in the model
implementation review, such as whether relationships and data properties are structured appropriately

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.4 What to look for
As you move through the book, consider the following:

2.4.1 Class model

Are the major classes appropriate for the recreation domain?
Are subclass relationships correct and understandable?
Are any classes missing, redundant, or overly broad?



2.4.2 Object properties

Do relationships connect the correct domain and range classes?
Are any relationship names unclear or inconsistent?
Are inverse-style relationships necessary and well named?



2.4.3 Datatype properties

Do the datatype properties reflect realistic operational attributes?
Are domains and ranges appropriately assigned?
Should any datatype properties instead be modeled differently?



2.4.4 Integration readiness

Does the model appear suitable for alignment with GIS, SDSFIE, and source vocabularies?
Are external concepts and references used in a clear and manageable way?

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.5 Site organization
This book is organized into the following sections:

Ontology Overview — conceptual summary of the ontology structure
Ontology Visualization — interactive graph views of class and property relationships
Spatial Diagrams — supporting spatial context
SDSFIE Crosswalk — comparison artifact for review and traceability

title: 2  Introduction
text:
2.6 Expected use
This site is meant to support iterative review. It should be easy to update as ontology files and supporting materials evolve.

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.1 Scope
The recreation ontology models core recreation business entities, managed places, facilities, amenities, services, programs, fees, and related operational concepts relevant to USACE recreation management.
The primary ontology source for this chapter is:

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text: data/02-recreation-ontology.ttl

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.2 High-level structure
At a high level, the ontology is organized around several major concept areas:

Domain entities that represent the broad recreation business space
Physical and managed assets such as recreation areas, facilities, and amenities
Water-access and linear assets for specialized physical features
Visitor services and programs representing operational and service concepts
Fees, agreements, incidents, and activities representing supporting business concepts

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.3 Class hierarchy patterns
The ontology uses rdfs:subClassOf to establish class hierarchies. These hierarchies help distinguish:

general versus specialized recreation concepts
managed places versus supporting amenities
physical assets versus operational/service concepts

Examples of expected hierarchy patterns include:

broad domain classes that act as abstract parents
facility subclasses for more specialized recreation facilities
amenity subclasses for supporting infrastructure
specialized trail, campground, and aquatic-use concepts

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.4 Property model
The ontology includes both object properties and datatype properties.

3.4.1 Object properties
Object properties connect one resource to another resource. In this ontology, they are used to express relationships such as:

an area having a facility
a facility having an amenity
an asset supporting an activity
an entity being managed under a program

These relationships are especially important for understanding how the model expresses operational structure rather than just classification.


3.4.2 Datatype properties
Datatype properties connect a resource to a literal value such as text, number, date, or decimal. In this ontology, they are used for values such as:

local identifiers
facility names
counts of sites or slips
trail length
operating dates

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.5 External references
The ontology also connects to external namespaces and concepts, including:

SKOS concepts
XML Schema datatypes
OWL classes such as owl:Thing

These references help the ontology align with supporting vocabularies and standard semantic web constructs.

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.6 How to review this ontology
A useful way to review the ontology is to separate the work into three passes:

3.6.1 1. Hierarchy pass
Check whether classes are arranged appropriately and whether subclassing is intuitive.


3.6.2 2. Relationship pass
Review object properties and verify that domains, ranges, and property names make sense.


3.6.3 3. Attribute pass
Review datatype properties and confirm that literal-valued attributes are assigned to the right classes with the right datatypes.

title: 3  Ontology Overview
text:
3.7 Next step
The next chapter provides an interactive graph-based visualization of the ontology to support structural review.

title: 4  Ontology Module Review
text:
4.1 Executive Summary
This interactive page visualizes the ontology structure in 02-recreation-ontology.ttl.
It provides four separate views:

title: 4  Ontology Module Review
text:
Full Graph
Hierarchy Only
Object Properties Only
Datatype Properties Only


4.1.1 Display behavior

Edge labels are suppressed by default in all views to improve readability
Hover over edges to inspect relationship names
Hover over nodes to inspect labels, groups, and full URIs



4.1.2 How to use this page

Use the view selector to switch between graph modes
Pan/Zoom to navigate dense clusters
Hover over nodes and edges to inspect labels, URIs, and relationship types
Click a node to highlight immediate relationships
Use the node selector within each graph to jump to a resource


Select graph view:

Full Graph
Hierarchy Only
Object Properties Only
Datatype Properties Only

title: 4  Ontology Module Review
text:
4.2 Notes
This page is designed specifically for 02-recreation-ontology.ttl.

4.2.1 Graph views

Full Graph combines hierarchy, object-property, and datatype-property relationships
Hierarchy Only shows only rdfs:subClassOf
Object Properties Only shows domain --property--> range
Datatype Properties Only shows domain --property--> datatype



4.2.2 Node categories

ontology classes
superclasses
XSD datatypes
external SKOS concepts
external classes such as owl:Thing

This design avoids cross-view leakage by rendering separate graph widgets for each mode.

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.1 Ontology Scope
These diagrams describe the active spatial extension in 06-recreation-spatial.ttl. The spatial ontology models GIS-recordable feature representations; it does not replace recreation business entities, source vocabulary concepts, or geometry instances.
flowchart LR
    RECS["recs: Recreation Spatial Ontology"]
    REC["rec: Recreation Domain Ontology"]
    USF["usf: Spatial Foundation"]
    UPR["upr: Physical Realms"]
    GEO["GeoSPARQL"]

    RECS -->|imports| REC
    RECS -->|imports| USF
    RECS -->|imports| UPR
    RECS -->|imports| GEO
    RECS --> RSF["recs:RecreationSpatialFeature"]

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.2 Core Spatial Contract





classDiagram
    class GeoFeature
    class SpatialFeature
    class RecreationSpatialFeature {
        <<owl:Class>>
        +sourceLayerName
        +featureGrain
        +sourceIdentifier
    }
    class RecreationDomainEntity
    class SourceConcept
    class Geometry
    class PhysicalRealm
    class SpatialPattern

    GeoFeature <|-- RecreationSpatialFeature
    SpatialFeature <|-- RecreationSpatialFeature
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> RecreationDomainEntity : representsEntity
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> SourceConcept : representsConcept
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> Geometry : geo_hasGeometry
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> PhysicalRealm : intersectsPhysicalRealm
    RecreationSpatialFeature --> SpatialPattern : usesSpatialPattern

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.3 Representation Relationship





flowchart LR
    Entity["rec:RecreationDomainEntity"]
    Feature["recs:RecreationSpatialFeature"]
    Geometry["geo:Geometry"]
    Concept["skos:Concept"]

    Entity -->|rec:hasSpatialRepresentation| Feature
    Feature -->|recs:representsEntity| Entity
    Feature -->|geo:hasGeometry| Geometry
    Feature -->|recs:representsConcept| Concept

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.4 Spatial Pattern Model





flowchart LR
    Pattern["recs:usesSpatialPattern"]
    Direct["recs:DirectFeaturePattern"]
    Representation["recs:RepresentationFeaturePattern"]
    Feature["all current recs feature classes"]

    Feature --> Pattern
    Pattern --> Representation
    Pattern -. available but not currently used .-> Direct

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.9 Source Concept And Boundary Records
{mermaid} flowchart LR LCF[“LandClassificationAreaFeaturesf:Polygon”] –> LC[“skos:Conceptland allocation or classification”] WZF[“WaterSurfaceZoneFeaturesf:Polygon”] –> WZ[“skos:Conceptwater surface zoning”] PBF[“ProjectBoundaryFeaturesf:Polygon”] –> PB[“source boundary conceptimplementation-specific”]
LCF -->|recs:representsConcept| LC
WZF -->|recs:representsConcept| WZ
PBF -->|recs:representsConcept when source-backed| PB

## Physical Realm Context

{mermaid}
flowchart LR
    Feature["recs:RecreationSpatialFeature"]
    Land["upr:LandRealm"]
    Water["upr:AquaticSurface"]
    Interface["land-water interface"]

    Feature -->|recs:intersectsPhysicalRealm| Land
    Feature -->|recs:intersectsPhysicalRealm| Water
    Land --- Interface
    Water --- Interface
    Interface --> BoatRamp["BoatRampFeature"]
    Interface --> Marina["MarinaFeature"]
    Interface --> FishingPier["FishingPierFeature"]
    Interface --> SwimmingArea["SwimmingAreaFeature"]

text:

{mermaid} flowchart LR Layer[“source layer or feature class”] Row[“source record”] Feature[“recs:RecreationSpatialFeature”] Entity[“rec:RecreationDomainEntity”] Concept[“skos:Concept”] Geometry[“geo:Geometry”]
Layer -->|recs:sourceLayerName| Feature
Row -->|recs:sourceIdentifier| Feature
Feature -->|recs:featureGrain| Row
Feature -->|recs:representsEntity| Entity

```

title: 5  Recreation Spatial Diagrams
text:
5.12 Property Matrix










Property
Type
Domain
Range
Role




recs:representsEntity
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
rec:RecreationDomainEntity
Links a GIS representation to the managed recreation entity it represents.


recs:representsConcept
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
skos:Concept
Links a GIS representation to a source-backed classification, zoning, or boundary concept.


rec:hasSpatialRepresentation
Object property
rec:RecreationDomainEntity
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
Inverse of recs:representsEntity.


recs:usesSpatialPattern
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
owl:Thing
Declares whether the feature is modeled as a representation or direct feature pattern.


recs:intersectsPhysicalRealm
Object property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
upr:PhysicalRealm
Connects a spatial feature to land, water, or other physical realm context.


recs:sourceLayerName
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Preserves the source layer or feature class name.


recs:featureGrain
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Explains what one source row or feature represents.


recs:sourceIdentifier
Datatype property
recs:RecreationSpatialFeature
xsd:string
Preserves the source record identifier or crosswalk key.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.1 Purpose
Status: v2 review draft Date: 2026-06-01 Sources: Recreation v2 artifacts; SDSFIE 5.0 Gold Draft vocabulary, ontology, and enumerations
This crosswalk records where SDSFIE terminology can be mapped cleanly to the recreation ontology without making SDSFIE’s feature model govern the recreation conceptual model.
The recreation model keeps these boundaries:

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
rec:* classes are business/domain concepts.
recs:*Feature classes are GIS representation classes.
recv:* concepts preserve recreation source vocabulary.
sd:* and sde:* terms are SDSFIE alignment anchors.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.2 Mapping Semantics
Predicates are written from the recreation term to the SDSFIE anchor.







Predicate
Meaning in this crosswalk




skos:exactMatch
The recreation term and SDSFIE anchor can be treated as equivalent for the mapped feature concept.


skos:closeMatch
The concepts are operationally close, but differ by model grain, feature/entity boundary, or implementation convention.


skos:broadMatch
The SDSFIE anchor is broader than the recreation term.


skos:narrowMatch
The SDSFIE anchor is narrower than the recreation term.


skos:relatedMatch
The terms are relevant to one another but should not be treated as a hierarchy or equivalence.


No mapping
No class-level SDSFIE anchor is clear enough to assert by default.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.3 Spatial Feature Crosswalk










Recreation term
Recreation meaning
SDSFIE anchor
Mapping
Notes




recs:RecreationAreaFeature
GIS representation of rec:RecreationArea
sd:RecreationBoundary
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE’s boundary represents the overall recreation area. Recreation keeps the managed area entity separate from its boundary feature.


recs:RecreationFacilityFeature
Generic facility representation
sd:RecreationFeature
skos:broadMatch
SDSFIE RecreationFeature is a general storage class for recreation objects and assets.


recs:RecreationAmenityFeature
Generic amenity representation
sd:RecreationFeature
skos:broadMatch
Amenity is a clearer recreation role than the flat SDSFIE feature bucket.


recs:CampgroundFeature
Campground spatial representation
sd:Campground
skos:exactMatch
SDSFIE also allows the campground recreation feature type value when using the generic feature class.


recs:CampsiteFeature
Individual campsite representation
sd:Campsite
skos:exactMatch
SDSFIE campsite use values can be implementation attributes, not recreation OWL subclasses by default.


recs:DayUseAreaFeature
Managed day-use area representation
sd:RecreationFeature
skos:broadMatch
SDSFIE has no clean day-use-area class-level anchor.


recs:PicnicAreaFeature
Picnic area representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_picnicSite
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE models this as a recreation feature type value rather than a standalone class.


recs:MarinaFeature
Managed boating facility representation
sd:Marina
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE marina is a harbor facility. Recreation marina is a managed recreation facility and water access asset.


recs:WaterAccessFeature
Generic water access representation
sd:RecreationFeature
skos:relatedMatch
Water access is a recreation facet that can cut across several SDSFIE classes and values.


recs:BoatRampFeature
Recreational boat launch representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_boatRamp
skos:closeMatch
Do not map recreational boat ramps to sd:BoatRamp; SDSFIE scopes that feature class to non-recreational ramps.


recs:FishingPierFeature
Fishing platform or pier representation
sd:FishingFeature
skos:broadMatch
SDSFIE fishing feature is broader than a fishing pier.


recs:FishingPierFeature
Fishing platform or pier representation
sd:DockOrWharf
skos:relatedMatch
Useful when the source feature is also managed as dock or wharf infrastructure.


recs:FuelDockFeature
Marine fuel service asset representation
sd:DockOrWharf
skos:broadMatch
SDSFIE has the generic water-land interface structure; fuel service is the narrower recreation asset role.


recs:SwimmingAreaFeature
Designated swimming area representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_swimmingDesignatedBeach
skos:closeMatch
Close when the source feature is a designated beach.


recs:SwimmingAreaFeature
Designated swimming area representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_waterActivitySite
skos:broadMatch
Use for broader water activity site implementations.


recs:TrailFeature
Managed recreation route representation
sd:RecreationTrail
skos:exactMatch
Trail use values remain attributes or source concepts unless separate classes are justified.


recs:ParkingAreaFeature
Recreation parking area representation
sd:ParkingArea
skos:exactMatch
SDSFIE explicitly separates parking area mapping from pavement management segmentation.


recs:RestroomFeature
Restroom representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_restroom
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE restroom value is flush-oriented. Vault toilets map separately.


recs:VisitorCenterFeature
Visitor information and interpretation facility representation
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_museumOrVisitorCenter
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE combines museums and visitor centers under one feature type value.


recs:LandClassificationAreaFeature
Source-backed land classification area
sd:LandUse
skos:relatedMatch
SDSFIE land use is related, but not identical to USACE recreation land allocation or classification concepts.


recs:WaterSurfaceZoneFeature
Source-backed water surface zone
sd:RestrictedArea
skos:relatedMatch
Applies only when the zone restricts access or activity. Other water-surface zones require source-specific mapping.


recs:ProjectBoundaryFeature
Project boundary representation
No default mapping
No mapping
A project boundary is not automatically a SDSFIE recreation boundary. Map to sd:RecreationBoundary only when the source explicitly represents a recreation area boundary.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
section: 6.4 Domain Terms Without Current Spatial Feature Classes
text:
6.4 Domain Terms Without Current Spatial Feature Classes
These recreation domain classes have useful SDSFIE anchors, but the active spatial extension does not currently define separate recs:*Feature classes for each one.









Recreation term
SDSFIE anchor
Mapping
Notes




rec:PicnicShelter
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_picnicShelter
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE models picnic shelter as a recreation feature type value.


rec:Playground
sd:Playground
skos:closeMatch
The SDSFIE class is spatial/entity oriented; recreation keeps playground as an amenity domain class.


rec:BoatSlip
sd:DockOrWharf
skos:relatedMatch
A slip is a managed mooring location, not necessarily the dock or wharf structure itself.


rec:PumpOutStation
sd:DockOrWharf
skos:relatedMatch
Related when the station is located on dock infrastructure; no default equivalence.


rec:Restroom
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_restroom; sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_vaultToilet
skos:narrowMatch
SDSFIE separates narrower restroom feature type values below the broader recreation restroom domain class.


rec:FlushRestroom
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_restroom
skos:exactMatch
SDSFIE restroom definition is flush-water oriented.


rec:VaultToilet
sde:RecreationFeatureUseType_vaultToilet
skos:exactMatch
SDSFIE has a specific vault toilet recreation feature type value.


rec:Trail
sde:RecreationTrailUseType_hike; sde:RecreationTrailUseType_bike; sde:RecreationTrailUseType_equestrianOrPack
skos:narrowMatch
SDSFIE trail use values are narrower than the general managed trail class.


rec:HikingTrail
sde:RecreationTrailUseType_hike
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE trail use value can type a recs:TrailFeature implementation.


rec:BikeTrail
sde:RecreationTrailUseType_bike
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE trail use value can type a recs:TrailFeature implementation.


rec:EquestrianTrail
sde:RecreationTrailUseType_equestrianOrPack
skos:closeMatch
SDSFIE combines equestrian and pack trail use.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.5 SDSFIE Anchors To Use Carefully







SDSFIE term
Crosswalk decision




sd:BoatRamp
Do not use as the recreational boat ramp match. SDSFIE scopes the class to non-recreational boat ramps and directs recreational ramps to sd:RecreationFeature with the boat ramp type value.


sd:RecreationFeature
Use as a broad anchor for generic recreation assets, not as a replacement for rec:RecreationFacility, rec:RecreationAmenity, or rec:WaterAccessAsset.


sd:RecreationBoundary
Use for overall recreation area boundaries, not for every recreation use area or project boundary.


sd:DockOrWharf
Use for dock/wharf infrastructure. Do not collapse boat slips, fuel docks, pump-out stations, and fishing piers into this class unless the source feature grain supports that interpretation.


sd:LandUse
Related to recreation land classification, but not equivalent to USACE source classification vocabularies.


sd:RestrictedArea
Related to water-surface zones only when the zone restricts access or activity.

title: 6  Recreation SDSFIE Crosswalk
text:
6.6 Implementation Rule
Use SDSFIE mappings as crosswalk assertions and migration aids. Do not make SDSFIE classes superclasses of recreation classes unless an implementation profile explicitly requires that inheritance.
